source.title: Design and application of a lactulose biosensor

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The study aims to engineer the Escherichia coli LacI repressor to specifically respond to lactulose, a prebiotic disaccharide, enabling the construction of a whole-cell biosensor for high-throughput screening of lactulose-producing enzymes. Existing lactulose biosensors are in vitro and enzyme-based; an in vivo transcription factor-based biosensor is desired for metabolic engineering applications.

section_gists.heading: Design and application of a lactulose biosensor
section_gists.gist: The paper introduces the need for customized small-molecule biosensors and the potential of engineering LacI effector specificity. Lactulose is an important prebiotic. Previous lactulose biosensors are in vitro and enzyme-based. The goal is to engineer LacI to respond to lactulose and apply it as a whole-cell biosensor for directed evolution of cellobiose 2-epimerase (C2E) to enhance lactulose production.
process_signals: Problem: lack of regulatory proteins for desired small molecules limits biosensor applications.
Hypothesis: LacI effector-binding pocket can be engineered to recognize lactulose.
Motivation: need for in vivo biosensor for lactulose to enable high-throughput screening.
failures_or_limits: Existing lactulose biosensors are in vitro and not suitable for in vivo high-throughput screening.

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: A saturation mutagenesis library targeting four residues (I79, F161, N246, L296) in the LacI effector-binding pocket was constructed and screened via FACS dual screening (positive with lactulose, negative with lactose or no inducer). Mutant LacI-L5 (F161K) showed ~2.7-fold induction by lactulose, with no response to other disaccharides. SPR confirmed binding (KD=17 mM). LacI-L5 retained IPTG response but lactose anti-inducer effect was similar to wild-type. The biosensor was used to screen a C2E random mutagenesis library, yielding mutant C2 (E272D, F339S) with ~22-fold increased lactulose production, mainly due to ~32-fold higher expression level, not increased specific activity.
process_signals: Screening: 4 rounds of FACS dual screening.
Induction: 2.7-fold by lactulose; half-maximal at 0.13 mM.
Specificity: no induction by lactose, maltose, sucrose, cellobiose, melibiose, epilactose.
Binding: KD=17 mM for lactulose; no binding by wild-type.
Application: C2E mutant C2 (E272D, F339S) showed 22-fold lactulose increase; 32-fold expression increase; 1.3-fold specific activity increase at 37°C.
methods: Saturation mutagenesis at I79, F161, N246, L296.
FACS dual screening: positive with 10 mM lactulose; negative with 10 mM lactose or no inducer.
C2E random mutagenesis library co-expressed with LacI-L5 biosensor.
failures_or_limits: C2E mutant improvement mainly from expression, not catalytic efficiency.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: Wild-type LacI has stringent specificity for allolactose. Engineering specificity is challenging; LacI-L5 shows good specificity for lactulose over similar disaccharides. IPTG response could not be eliminated. The biosensor successfully enabled high-throughput screening of C2E mutants, revealing that increased expression, not codon bias, drove lactulose overproduction. The work demonstrates potential for engineering LacI for various small-molecule sensors and gene switches.
process_signals: Challenge: high induction specificity is difficult to achieve.
IPTG response persists despite negative screening.
C2E mutant improvement attributed to expression level, not codon usage.
Potential for broader applications of engineered LacI.
failures_or_limits: Specificity limited to tested disaccharides; broader specificity not explored.
Mechanism of expression increase not fully elucidated.

section_gists.heading: Methods
section_gists.gist: Strain BLGR (ΔlacI, ΔgalK, ΔrecA) constructed. Plasmids: pLac7 (tac-gfpuv), pLac7-L5 (mutant LacI), pN25-C2E (C2E expression). LacI saturation library constructed by overlap extension PCR targeting residues 79, 161, 246, 296; MEGAWHOP cloning; 1.08×10^6 transformants. Library transformed into BLGR; FACS screening: negative (no inducer) then positive (lactulose) and negative (lactose) rounds. Details of C2E library screening, protein purification, SPR, HPLC, Western blot provided.
process_signals: Library construction: overlap extension PCR, MEGAWHOP, DpnI digestion.
FACS: 405 nm excitation, 530/30 emission; first round negative (no inducer) collected 80% least fluorescent cells.
Screening strategy: eliminate leaky expression and lactose-responsive mutants.
methods: Strain construction: P1 transduction, FLP recombination.
Library construction: overlap extension PCR, MEGAWHOP.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The research follows a linear directed evolution workflow: (1) identify target residues in LacI binding pocket, (2) create saturation mutagenesis library, (3) screen for lactulose-responsive mutants using FACS dual selection, (4) characterize best mutant specificity and affinity, (5) apply biosensor to screen C2E library for improved lactulose production.
The choice of residues I79, F161, N246, L296 was based on crystal structure with IPTG, hypothesizing that mutations there could alter effector specificity while maintaining allosteric function.
The dual screening strategy (positive for lactulose, negative for lactose and no inducer) was designed to ensure specificity and low background.

The successful application to C2E engineering validates the biosensor's utility, but the main improvement came from expression level rather than catalytic activity, suggesting the biosensor can select for overall productivity.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Why did the F161K mutation confer lactulose specificity? Structural basis not discussed.
Could further mutagenesis improve affinity and eliminate IPTG response?
What is the dynamic range and detection limit of the biosensor in practical applications?
How generalizable is this approach to other disaccharides or small molecules?
What caused the increased expression of C2E mutant? mRNA stability, protein stability, or other factors?

events.summary: LacI site-directed saturation library constructed targeting residues 79, 161, 246, 296 using overlap extension PCR and MEGAWHOP cloning.
FACS dual screening performed: negative screen without inducer to eliminate leaky expression, negative screen with lactose, positive screen with lactulose, repeated four times.
C2E random mutagenesis library constructed via error-prone PCR and MEGAWHOP, yielding ~1×10^6 transformants with average 3 mutations per clone.
numbers_or_metrics: average of 3 nucleotide mutations per clone

Western blot analysis performed to compare expression levels of wild-type C2E and mutant C2.
Specific activities of wild-type C2E and mutant C2 measured at 37°C and 80°C.

Therefore, the C2E mutants obtained in this study have potential applications in the industrial production of this molecule.
As a whole-cell biosensor, strain BLGR harboring plasmid pLac7-L5 was successfully used for high-throughput screening of the C2E mutagenesis library for lactulose hyper-producers.
The pN25-C2E plasmid expressing mutant C2 was isolated and retransformed into strain BLGR, and a significantly elevated expression level of the mutant C2E was confirmed.
HPLC analyses verified enhanced lactulose production by these selected variants compared with that of the strain expressing wild-type C2E.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Engineering LacI repressor to specifically respond to lactulose for use as a whole-cell biosensor to enable high-throughput screening of lactulose-producing enzymes.

canonical_events.summary: Lack of regulatory proteins for desired small molecules limits biosensor applications; existing lactulose biosensors are in vitro and not suitable for in vivo high-throughput screening.
LacI effector-binding pocket can be engineered to recognize lactulose, enabling a customized small-molecule biosensor.

Site-directed saturation library constructed targeting four residues (I79, F161, N246, L296) in LacI effector-binding pocket using overlap extension PCR and MEGAWHOP cloning, yielding 1.08×10^6 transformants.
FACS dual screening performed: negative screen without inducer to eliminate leaky expression, negative screen with lactose, positive screen with lactulose; repeated four times.
Mutant LacI-L5 (F161K) identified: ~2.7-fold induction by lactulose, half-maximal at 0.13 mM, no response to other disaccharides (lactose, maltose, sucrose, cellobiose, melibiose, epilactose).
SPR confirmed LacI-L5 binds lactulose with KD=17 mM; wild-type LacI shows no binding.
LacI-L5 remained responsive to IPTG induction, though less than wild-type; IPTG response could not be eliminated.
C2E random mutagenesis library constructed via error-prone PCR and MEGAWHOP, yielding ~1×10^6 transformants with average 3 mutations per clone.
key_numbers: average of 3 nucleotide mutations per clone

canonical_events.summary: C2E library screened by co-expressing with LacI-L5 biosensor on LB agar with 10 mM lactose; colonies with stronger GFP fluorescence selected.
Mutant C2 (E272D, F339S) showed ~22-fold increase in lactulose production at 37°C; HPLC confirmed enhanced production.
Western blot revealed ~32-fold higher expression level of mutant C2 compared to wild-type C2E; specific activity only 1.3-fold higher at 37°C (11 U/mg vs 14 U/mg).
Increased expression of C2 mutant not due to codon usage bias; mechanism of expression increase not fully elucidated.
LacI-L5 induction fold moderate (2.7-fold), binding affinity weak (KD=17 mM), and IPTG response persists; C2E improvement mainly from expression, not catalytic efficiency.
Whole-cell biosensor successfully applied for high-throughput screening of C2E library, demonstrating potential for engineering LacI for various small-molecule sensors.

edges.reason: Problem motivates hypothesis to engineer LacI for lactulose recognition.
Hypothesis leads to design of saturation library targeting effector-binding pocket.
Library construction enables FACS screening for lactulose-responsive mutants.
Screening identifies LacI-L5 with desired specificity and induction profile.
SPR validates binding affinity of LacI-L5 for lactulose.
Despite specificity, LacI-L5 retains IPTG response, a limitation.
Characterized biosensor enables design of C2E library for screening.
C2E library construction enables screening with LacI-L5 biosensor.
Screening identifies C2 mutant with enhanced lactulose production.
Further analysis reveals expression level, not specific activity, drives improvement.
Analysis shows codon bias not responsible for expression increase.
IPTG limitation contributes to overall biosensor limitations.
Expression-driven improvement highlights limitation in catalytic efficiency gain.
Despite limitations, biosensor application validates overall approach.

must_cover_points: Problem of lacking regulatory proteins for small molecules
Hypothesis to engineer LacI for lactulose
Design of saturation library at four residues
Identification and characterization of LacI-L5 (specificity, affinity, IPTG limitation)
Identification of C2 mutant with enhanced production
Limitations of biosensor (moderate induction, weak affinity, IPTG response)
